# Questions in Livestream 148

Live Stream #148: Christian Marriage, Meant To Endure

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:50</a> | Does John 20:12 suggest Jesus is the Ark of the Covenant? | He says the scene uses ark imagery, but the point is not that Jesus is the ark; rather, Jesus fills the Shekinah-presence typology. | He points to the two angels at the head and foot of where Jesus' body had been and compares that arrangement to the cherubim associated with the ark. The typological point is that the presence is no longer between them: just as the Shekinah glory left the ark to fill the temple, the risen Jesus is no longer lying between the angels. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=909" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:09</a> | How should we picture the "walls of water" in Exodus 14:22? | He argues the image is often exaggerated; instead of sheer vertical walls, he pictures slanted, wind-driven boundaries. | He says "wall" need not mean two vertical, ten-story walls like a movie image. He connects the term to a sloped boundary or demarcation, with wind pushing back a body of water perhaps 20 to 40 feet deep, making angled boundaries more plausible than narrow towers of water. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=1475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:35</a> | What are your thoughts on getting a bachelor's at Boyce College? | He says to evaluate the program according to goals, quality, and future academic plans rather than name alone. | He thinks Boyce is stronger for master's or M.Div. work than for a bachelor's degree. For many students he would choose a community college, state school, or secular college first because it may be cheaper, broader, and less duplicative before seminary graduate work, while recognizing that some students may have good reasons to avoid a secular environment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=1681" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:01</a> | What are your thoughts on the Ku Klux Klan? | He condemns the Klan and treats it as morally and historically indefensible. | The transcript segment immediately before the listed row treats the Klan as a white-supremacy organization and discusses it negatively as a political and social evil. His answer is not a nuanced defense of any part of the Klan; it is a rejection of the organization and what it represents. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=1881" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:21</a> | What are Leviathan and Behemoth? | He says they are not easy one-to-one animal identifications and should be read in their poetic and ancient Near Eastern contexts. | He describes Leviathan and Behemoth in classical terms as chaos monsters that may be patterned partly on real animals. He mentions suggestions such as hippos, crocodiles, aurochs, and dinosaur readings, but rejects certainty and says the main shared point is that they are monsters God conquers or subdues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=2093" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:53</a> | Was Jonah's fish actually a fish? | He says probably not in the modern taxonomic sense; the Hebrew wording can mean a great sea creature. | He gives two possible readings: a real sea creature, probably a whale understood as a fish in ancient Israelite taxonomy, or a personified chaos monster/current pulling Jonah into the deep. He does not press a single view, but says either a whale-like creature or a water-chaos image could fit the story's language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=2414" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:14</a> | What is baptism for the dead in 1 Corinthians 15:25-30? | He says the passage is difficult and should not be made into a major doctrine beyond Paul's resurrection argument. | He explains it as a practice some Corinthian Christians apparently performed for people who had died without baptism, hoping the dead person would receive afterlife benefits. Paul is not endorsing the practice as effective; he is using it rhetorically to expose inconsistency if the Corinthians practice baptism for the dead while denying bodily resurrection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=2697" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:57</a> | What are your thoughts on Buddhism, Sikhism, and Hinduism? | He gives broad comments and treats them as distinct traditions rather than one interchangeable category. | He gives a rapid overview rather than a full treatment. He frames Buddhism around the path to enlightenment, reincarnation, and loss of individual identity in Nirvana; Hinduism as pantheistic and reincarnation-based; and Sikhism as a separate, complicated philosophical religion with its own practices, texts, and internal variety. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=3107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:47</a> | How well would Moses have known Ramesses II if Moses grew up in the royal court? | He says the question depends on chronology and the Exodus pharaoh identification, so it cannot be answered simply. | On his chronology, Moses did not grow up with Ramesses II because Ramesses had not yet been born when Moses left Egypt. He says Moses would more plausibly have known Seti I, Ramesses' father, and he also doubts Moses was trained as a warrior, describing him instead as more likely connected to scribal training. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=3224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:44</a> | Why doesn't ancient Egypt have a flood myth, and does that contradict the flood? | He says Egyptian tradition is shaped by the Nile and local worldview, so absence of a comparable flood myth is not decisive. | He says Egypt did have flood-related mythology, but its floods were annual Nile renewals rather than a great destructive flood story like those elsewhere. Egyptian creation traditions also include primordial waters, closer to Genesis 1 imagery, so the lack of a Genesis 6-7-style flood myth reflects Egypt's different relationship to the Nile rather than a decisive contradiction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=4165" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:25</a> | What is the typological significance of Joseph being named "the living one"? | He discusses Joseph typology cautiously and keeps it tied to the textual details rather than free association. | He parses Joseph's Egyptian name as carrying the sense that God has spoken and he lives. Joseph had been as good as dead in an Egyptian prison, so the naming marks him as the living one after a death sentence, which he treats as a type fulfilled in Christ, the living one who was dead and is alive forevermore in Revelation 1. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=4386" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:06</a> | What can we glean from Genesis 3:14? | He reads the verse in its curse and serpent context and warns against isolating it from the surrounding passage. | He focuses on the comparative construction, arguing that the serpent is compared with cattle and beasts as created beings without being reduced to an ordinary animal. The verse places the serpent under the curse in relation to the wider created order, with the high heavenly being cast down to the realm of dust and final judgment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=4829" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:29</a> | What are some bad practices of Ellen G. White? | He criticizes theological and interpretive practices associated with her work, especially where they override the text. | He distinguishes neutral practices such as dietary restrictions or Saturday Sabbath observance from making those practices mandatory for salvation. He criticizes Ellen G. White's prophetic authority claims, legalistic or judaizing tendencies in stricter Adventist circles, Ron Wyatt-style ark claims tied to her prophecy, and soul sleep as poor biblical interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=5731" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:31</a> | May I commission you to draw a sci-fi cover? | He says yes in principle, though his schedule and current commitments matter. | He says he appreciates the request but has many projects on his plate and notes that sci-fi cover work is not cheap. He does not make a firm commitment on stream, but invites the person to describe the book idea and continue the discussion on Discord. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=5797" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:37</a> | Why is "40 years" idiomatic in the Bible except during the wandering period? | He says it may be idiomatic in multiple places and each use has to be read in context. | He says the wandering period itself may also use 40 years idiomatically, though it appears to be close to that length based on his chronological markers. He compares 40 as a Levantine idiomatic number with 60 in Mesopotamian contexts and says the reason for choosing 40 is not well documented. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=6112" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:52</a> | Did other ancient civilizations have inflation, and what might American inflation look like after 3,000 years? | He says inflation and changing value systems have historical parallels, but the comparison is playful and limited. | He says ancient long-lived economies faced resource pressure as populations grew. Egypt hedged inflation through hegemony and imported supplies such as grain, while Akkadians, Assyrians, Babylonians, Hittites, Egyptians, and Romans used conquest, tribute, plunder, or currency systems to keep goods flowing back into the center. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=6850" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:10</a> | In Matthew 17:20, does the mountain refer to a kingdom and its god? | He says he cannot say much without deeper study and does not want to overstate the interpretation. | The available transcript support for this row is deliberately limited: he says he cannot comment much further until he has looked into the question more deeply. The safe expansion is therefore that he withholds a firm interpretation rather than treating the mountain-god or kingdom reading as established. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=6997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:37</a> | How should Christians study their Bible and do devotions? | He recommends regular reading, attention to context, and disciplined study rather than proof-texting. | He urges attentive reading: slow down, ask what words and images mean, and break down a passage rather than skimming it. He also recommends using commentaries carefully, growing in competence over time, and making prayer part of the discipline before or after reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=7414" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:34</a> | When does recognizing or admiring someone's attractiveness become lust? | He distinguishes noticing beauty from cultivating desire and treating another person as an object. | He says simply seeing or recognizing that someone is attractive is not lust. Lust begins when desire is directed toward someone or something one has no right to have, whether because the person is unavailable, married, not interested, or fictional; the key distinction is admiration versus covetous desire. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=8294" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:14</a> | What is the origin of the angels Michael and Gabriel, and how did Jews know their identities? | He points to Jewish textual traditions and later named-angel material rather than assuming the names are explained in early biblical texts. | He identifies Daniel as the primary source because both Michael and Gabriel are mentioned there, and he states that he holds to an early dating of Daniel. He rejects the need to make Enoch or Tobit the origin of the names and says Jews knew them through revelation given to Daniel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=8426" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:26</a> | What is the point of marriage? | He says marriage is covenantal and enduring, not merely about present romantic enjoyment. | He says one purpose of marriage is sanctification: two sinners must grow in Christlikeness to make the relationship work. He also distinguishes earthly conjugal marriage from enduring relationships, arguing that spouses will not have the same marital bond in heaven but may still carry that close relationship into eternity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=8673" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:33</a> | Does "until death do them part" mean remarriage is permitted after a spouse dies? | He says yes, the death of a spouse ends the marriage bond. | He says the institution and bond of marriage are temporal because they involve exclusive bodily and property rights in this world. When one spouse dies, that bond ends and the surviving spouse is free to remarry, even though the personal relationship with the deceased spouse is not erased in the afterlife. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=8952" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:29:12</a> | How would you respond to someone who says love is just an instinct or chemical reaction? | He says reductionistic accounts do not exhaust what love is, especially in Christian moral and covenantal terms. | He answers that even if love involves chemical reactions, that does not make it unreal; hunger is chemically mediated too. He rejects reductionism because human beings are physical and spiritual, so bodily processes can affect the spirit without explaining away the reality or moral meaning of love. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=9305" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:05</a> | Is the Sinai extension dangerous? | He says tourist zones like Sharm are considered safe, while deeper Sinai travel should be done with escorts rather than independently. | He says Sharm el-Sheikh is a well-protected resort area and on tourist-safe lists, but travel deeper into Sinai is more serious. A legitimate tour uses government-approved routes and armed escorts where required, while independent travel without escort is the danger he warns against. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=9867" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:27</a> | What is your best guess for where Sodom was? | He says his preferred location is in the southern Dead Sea region, while recognizing the debate. | He places Sodom near the southeast corner or southern tip of the Dead Sea, slightly inland to the east. His reasoning includes the location of tar pits and the attack route of the Mesopotamian coalition from Gaza, which he thinks better fits a southern site than a northern Dead Sea location. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=10042" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:22</a> | How did the Egyptian pantheon begin, and why did people believe in those gods? | He frames early Egyptian religion as developing from local cults, natural phenomena, and social-religious tradition. | He describes the early pantheon as developing from local animistic or anthropomorphic deities connected with regions and towns. As Egypt became more politically cohesive, these local gods were added into broader Upper and Lower Egyptian religious systems, though he says we cannot know exactly why Egyptians first adopted those forms of worship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=10241" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:41</a> | What is your overall view of dating, and do you have any advice? | He gives one main piece of advice: date with seriousness about marriage and shared faith. | His one major rule is not to date someone who is not a serious candidate for marriage. He specifically warns against dating someone already married or an unbeliever, saying missionary dating usually fails and may lead to a marriage with divided values, faith, and child-rearing commitments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=10467" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:27</a> | What if a spouse deconverts after marriage? | He says the marriage was entered in good faith and should be handled with covenant seriousness, not as an automatic escape clause. | He says if the marriage was entered in good faith and the deconverted spouse wants to stay, the Christian should stay and love that spouse through the situation. If the unbelieving spouse refuses to remain, there may be nothing the believer can do, but deconversion itself is not treated as an automatic reason to abandon the marriage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=10603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:43</a> | How should corporal-punishment passages like Proverbs 13:24 be read? | He insists the proverb be read with its paired verse and in disciplinary context, not as an isolated proof text for harshness. | He reads Proverbs 13:24 with 13:25, tying the rod to discipline that trains a child not to become a destructive glutton or irresponsible member of the community. He says "rod" can function as a metaphor for discipline generally, warns against abuse or anger-driven punishment, and frames the goal as loving correction for the child's future good. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=11183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:23</a> | What are your thoughts on Numbers 21:8-9 and the bronze serpent? | He discusses the bronze serpent as a divinely commanded healing sign, later misused when it became an object of improper devotion. | He notes two serpent terms in the passage, connecting the bronze image to a fiery serpent or seraph rather than merely an ordinary snake. In his reading, the image of a seraph-like heavenly being was raised so bitten Israelites could look in faith to God's provided sign and be healed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=11501" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:41</a> | What single book could convince skeptical fundamentalist Christians to look into ancient Near Eastern and Second Temple contexts? | He says there may not be one perfect book and recommends choosing resources that challenge the hermeneutic carefully. | He says the harder problem is not the lack of good background books but getting someone committed to a fundamentalist hermeneutic to read them. He mentions Baker's Old Testament background material as useful, but says the deeper issue is the "one interpretation" English-Bible-only mindset, which must be intrigued and educated slowly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=11791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:16:31</a> | How can someone get you to popcorn-review a particular video? | He says viewers cannot simply buy a popcorn review; those choices depend on his judgment and constraints. | He says suggestions are welcome in the comments rather than the live chat, but the team keeps popcorn-review choices under wraps. If they think a suggested video is good and appropriate, they may do it, but viewers cannot force or purchase a particular review. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=12074" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:14</a> | In Judges 3:8 and 3:10, has the Mesopotamian king been identified? | He says the identification is uncertain and discusses the limits of correlating the biblical name with external history. | He argues that "Mesopotamia" is a poor traditional translation and that the text is better tied to Aram in the post-Mitanni Syrian context. Because records from that early Judges-period Aramean setting are sparse, he does not identify the king in a king list, though he suggests an approximate 1150-1100 BC setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=12776" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:56</a> | Is there rhyme or reason to the ten plagues in Egypt? | He says yes; the plagues are not random and connect to Egyptian religious and natural-symbolic contexts. | In the transcript support for this row, he treats the plagues as ordered rather than arbitrary and consistent with his broader pattern of reading Exodus against Egyptian context. The expanded support here is limited in the available window, but the answer direction is that the plagues have a coherent Egyptian setting rather than being random punishments. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=12952" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:35:52</a> | How does the Israelite covenant compare with other ancient Near Eastern treaties? | He connects the covenant form to ancient treaty patterns while preserving the distinct biblical theology. | The transcript support for this row is brief in the inspected window, but it is consistent with his stated approach elsewhere in the stream: Israel's covenant can be compared with ancient treaty forms while still being read as theologically distinctive. He does not collapse the biblical covenant into a merely human treaty model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=13585" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:25</a> | In Genesis 31:53, are Nahor and Abraham worshiping the same God or separate gods? | He says the passage is complex and likely reflects family and regional divine language rather than a simple equivalence. | He explains the heap and pillar as covenant-witness stones, then notes that some interpreters see Abraham and Nahor as invoking separate gods because Abraham had been called away from his father's house. He treats the question as genuinely complex and examines the Hebrew rather than giving an easy one-line identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=13947" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:27</a> | Are justice and righteousness the same thing? | He says no; righteousness is right conduct and justice is a related but distinct moral-legal concept. | The transcript support for this row is limited in the inspected excerpts, but the row's short answer fits his distinction between moral rightness and judicial or social justice. He treats the terms as related ethical categories, not synonyms that can be flattened into one concept. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=14825" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:05</a> | In Matthew 5, is Christ correcting scripture or oral tradition? | He says Matthew 5 is addressing oral tradition, and that "enemy" in Matthew 5 is not being used exactly the same way as in Psalm 59. | He says Matthew 5 addresses an oral-tradition aphorism, "love your neighbor and hate your enemy," shaped by an us-versus-them mentality. He distinguishes ordinary enemies who wish harm from Psalm 59 enemies who are actively seeking bloodshed, and says love of enemies does not require letting others rob, kill, or force violence on you. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/MHfIQdIFROg?t=15816" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:23:36</a> | Would you consider doing a video on the Kenite-Midianite hypothesis? | He says yes in principle, calling it the best current working hypothesis, though he gives no promise on timing. | He says he would consider a video, while noting that most of the sources for the Kenite-Midianite hypothesis come from the Bible itself rather than abundant secondary evidence. He calls it a solid and currently best working hypothesis, better than a northern Levantine hypothesis, but makes no promise about timing or length. |
